authoritative_sources.title: Portfolio Manager: What data is required to benchmark your property?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA operates ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager and is authoritative for the minimum property and utility inputs needed before facilities benchmarking metrics are reportable.
used_for: evidence sufficiency for score and EUI reporting
scenario design for incomplete utility and property data

authoritative_sources.title: How the 1-100 ENERGY STAR Score is Calculated
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA is authoritative for the ENERGY STAR score methodology, making this source the public baseline for score-related metric logic and caveats.

authoritative_sources.title: The Difference Between Source and Site Energy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA's source-versus-site energy guidance is authoritative for denominator logic that materially changes EUI, benchmarking, and disclosure posture in facilities analytics.

authoritative_sources.title: National Operations & Maintenance Scope of Work
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA is an authoritative federal property operator. Its national O&M specification defines work-order classes, CMMS recordkeeping, contractor reporting, and service-response expectations directly relevant to facilities analytics.

authoritative_sources.title: Operations & Maintenance Best Practices Guide: Release 3.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Energy Federal Energy Management Program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOE FEMP is an authoritative federal facilities operations source. Its O&M guide defines maintenance planning, preventive versus reactive framing, and CMMS-centered evidence practices used in facilities reporting.

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General safety and health provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA publishes the controlling federal construction safety baseline, making it authoritative when hazards, inspections, or changed site conditions invalidate analytics outputs.
used_for: refusal triggers when changed conditions alter the answer
scenario design for inspection or hazard-driven escalations

authoritative_sources.title: Maintaining Your Property
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is an authority having jurisdiction. This official guide ties maintenance duties to permit and licensed-trade boundaries, defining where facilities analytics must stop and escalate.
used_for: licensed boiler, plumbing, and electrical boundary handling
refusal of unsupported permit or code judgments

authoritative_sources.title: What to Expect During an Inspection - Buildings
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB publishes official inspection workflow guidance, including failed inspection posture and correction handling, which is authoritative for completion-sensitive facilities reporting.

authoritative_sources.title: Special Inspections
authoritative_sources.publisher: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official inspection-program guidance provides a concrete public baseline for special inspection nomination, field reports, final letters, and the requirement that final letters be accepted before permit completion.

authoritative_sources.title: Commissioning Documents: Process, Contents, and Acceptance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG is a recognized federal and industry public building-guidance platform, and this commissioning reference is authoritative for systems manuals, issues logs, training, and retained documentation used in facilities closeout analytics.

authoritative_sources.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis, Selected Financial Data, and Supplementary Financial Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC KPI and MD&A guidance is the authoritative public baseline for defining, contextualizing, and caveating metrics when facilities analytics are used in external or investor-style disclosure contexts.
used_for: confidence and caveat requirements for audience-facing outputs

workflow_stages.name: Source collection and qualification
workflow_stages.description: Collect work-order, permit, inspection, site-log, utility, benchmark, subcontractor, safety, and closeout records and determine whether the evidence set is current and sufficient for analytics.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Metric definition and source hierarchy
workflow_stages.description: Resolve metric logic, denominator rules, date windows, benchmark assumptions, meter boundaries, and system precedence before analytical or narrative claims are assembled.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Facilities evidence reconciliation
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile CMMS, BAS or BMS, utility, permit, inspection, safety, subcontractor, and closeout records without collapsing conflicts or changed-condition signals.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Analysis or reporting package assembly
workflow_stages.description: Build trend notes, variance summaries, benchmark views, readiness indicators, and exception packages while preserving site, asset, permit, inspection, and closeout context.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Narrative synthesis and adjacent-authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Check that no unsupported compliance, completion, benchmark, causal, savings, or signoff language appears and escalate when permit, inspection, safety, or trade authority changes the answer.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Public metric-definition, benchmarking, or disclosure guidance

artifacts: work orders, service requests, PM plans, backlog records, and priority classifications
asset hierarchies, location hierarchies, BAS or BMS trends, utility bills, meter reads, and benchmarking extracts
permits, approved plans, permit status records, inspection requests, inspection reports, deficiency notices, and AHJ correspondence
daily logs, technician notes, field photos, safety observations, stop-work notices, and installation or test evidence
subcontractor updates, schedule records, change orders, contractor proposals, and callback history
closeout trackers, special inspection field reports, final letters, O&M manuals, training records, warranties, and turnover packages
owner reporting templates, tenant KPI dictionaries, benchmark definitions, and disclosure approval notes
systems_of_record: BAS or BMS and fault-management systems

allowed: Classify facilities analytics requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Define or validate metrics, source hierarchy, denominator logic, refresh assumptions, and caveat language from facilities records.
Prepare analytical briefs, reconciliation notes, and disclosure-risk escalations without overstating permit, code, inspection, signoff, or closeout authority.
escalate: Required tenant facts, record locations, metric definitions, benchmark methodology, or source precedence rules are missing and materially change the output.
Permit status, inspection posture, safety incidents, stop-work conditions, or closeout blockers materially change the analytical answer.
Public guidance, jurisdiction rules, or tenant policy conflict on metric eligibility, readiness, completion, benchmark, or disclosure language.
Requests cross into project controls ownership, superintendent action ownership, legal interpretation, design authority, or licensed trade signoff.
refuse: Requests to imply permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, engineer-of-record judgment, final inspection acceptance, or owner turnover approval.
Requests to publish unsupported causal, benchmark, policy, compliance, energy-savings, or final-completion claims not supported by the evidence set.
Requests to approve change orders, direct subcontractor work, negotiate with inspectors, or override safety, permit, inspection, or closeout blockers.

domain_failure_modes: Publishing PM compliance, response-time, downtime, or benchmark metrics even though denominator logic is disputed across CMMS, BAS or BMS, and tenant policy records.
Treating stale utility, permit, inspection, subcontractor, or closeout records as current and overstating readiness or completion.
Treating a closed work order as inspected, accepted, code-compliant, or closeout-complete without explicit downstream evidence.
Turning facilities analytics into project-controls, superintendent, permitting, safety, legal, or licensed-trade authority instead of staying inside evidence-backed analysis.
Ignoring safety observations or failed inspections that materially alter whether a trend or disclosure conclusion can be published confidently.
Producing generic dashboard language that drops work-order class, asset, permit, inspection, subcontractor, and closeout semantics specific to facilities operations.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Different systems disagree on the metric outcome
A stakeholder asks for stronger conclusions than the data supports

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit and inspection record naming
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Permit cards, signoff labels, special-inspection workflows, and final-completion terminology vary by authority having jurisdiction. Tenants must supply the local permit and inspection mapping used by their facilities portfolio.

EPA and SEC sources anchor the public baseline for benchmark methodology and disclosure discipline, but private-owner, lender, investor, or local law reporting obligations still require tenant publication policy and approval-chain context.

notes: Authoritative records remain the tenant-designated CMMS, BAS or BMS, utility, permitting, inspection, safety, project-management, and closeout systems constrained by the public baseline sources listed above.

The lane stops before permit approval, inspection signoff, code interpretation, licensed-trade judgment, final turnover acceptance, and external disclosure approval even when the reporting request is operationally urgent.
